title: Guardrails module singleton: LazyLock<Mutex<Option<...>>> anti-pattern
description:
Current state: 

Desired state: 

Reason: 

**Dimension**: Architectural Alignment (Dependency Inversion) + Error Orchestration

**Location**: src/guardrails.rs:26-99 (global GUARDRAILS + wrapper fns)

**Violation**: The guardrails engine is exposed as a global LazyLock<Mutex<Option<GuardrailsEngine>>> accessed via free functions (check_file_access, record_file_modification, check_diff_thresholds, etc.). This is a textbook DIP violation:
- Callers cannot inject a test double
- The module has HIDDEN global state — calling configure() at startup is a temporal-coupling requirement
- If configure() is not called, every wrapper silently no-ops (guardrails.rs:44-49) — violating 'fail fast at perimeters'
- Mutex locking on EVERY file access and modification is a hot-path synchronization cost; the guard only holds an Option read
- The Mutex itself is unnecessary because GuardrailsEngine is set once at startup and never mutated; an RwLock or std::sync::OnceLock would be more appropriate
- Errors in lock acquisition (PoisonError) are silently swallowed (`if let Ok(guard) = ...`) — if any thread panics while holding the lock, ALL subsequent guardrail checks pass with Ok(()) silently

**Mandated Refactor**:
1. Replace with `OnceLock<GuardrailsEngine>` — set once, lock-free reads forever after.
2. Pass &GuardrailsEngine explicitly to call sites (via AppContext struct) instead of a global.
3. If global is unavoidable, at least return Result from each wrapper so an unconfigured engine is a loud panic/error, not silent allow.
4. Add a startup assertion: in main.rs, after guardrails::configure(), probe GUARDRAILS.lock() and panic if empty.
